segments.purgatorio-31-g33: This segment is a single period (punctuation) marking the end of the preceding sentence or a pause between speeches; it carries no lexical or grammatical content.
segments.purgatorio-31-g34: "Dopo" is the preposition 'after' introducing a temporal phrase. "la tratta" is a feminine noun meaning 'the drawing-out' or 'the drawn breath' (tratta = a drawn or stretched action) and is followed by the partitive/genitive contraction "d’" (di) + "un sospiro" = 'of a sigh'; "amaro" is an adjective 'bitter' modifying "sospiro." The whole phrase is an adverbial clause of time: 'After the drawn-out/bitter sigh.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g35: "A pena" is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'scarcely' or 'hardly' ('a' + 'pena' literally 'at difficulty'). "Ebbi" is passato remoto, first person singular of "avere," here 'I had' (i.e. 'I scarcely had'). "La voce che rispuose" is a subordinate clause: "la voce" (the voice) is the object and "che rispuose" ('that answered/replied') is a relative clause modifying "voce." The overall meaning: 'I could hardly summon/possess the voice that answered.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g36: "E" is the conjunction 'and' linking the next clause. "Le labbra" is the plural subject 'the lips,' so the verb "formaro" is third-person plural passato remoto ('they formed'). "A fatica" is an adverbial phrase 'with difficulty' modifying the verb. The clitic "la" refers back to "la voce" (feminine singular) as the direct object: 'and the lips with difficulty formed it.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g37: "Piangendo" is a gerund ('weeping') showing an action simultaneous with the main verb. "Dissi" is passato remoto, first person singular of "dire" ('I said'). The order (gerund + verb) emphasizes the manner or emotional state in which he spoke.
segments.purgatorio-31-g38: "Le presenti cose" = 'these present things' ("presenti" qualifying "cose"). "Col" is the contraction of "con + il" = 'with' or 'by the'; here it introduces the instrumental phrase "col falso lor piacer" meaning 'by their false pleasure/charm' ("lor" is the enclitic possessive 'their' placed after the adjective). "Volser" is a poetic/past form (passato remoto) meaning 'they turned' or 'they caused to turn' (from *volgere*); "miei passi" ('my steps') is the object indicating the direction/motion affected. Altogether: 'These present things, by their deceptive charm, turned my steps.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g39: "Tosto che" is a temporal conjunction meaning 'as soon as' or 'the moment that.' The sequence "’l vostro viso si nascose" uses the clitic "si" reflexively with "nascose" (passato remoto of nascondersi) to mean 'your face hid itself' i.e. 'your face was hidden/disappeared.' The clause gives the time when the preceding action occurred.
segments.purgatorio-31-g40: "Ed ella:" is simply the connective "and" + subject "she:" introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-31-g41: This conditional phrase "Se tacessi o se negassi ciò che confessi" uses two imperfect-subjunctive forms (tacessi, negassi) to state hypothetical or potential actions: "if I (or you) were to keep silent, or deny". "ciò che confessi" is a relative clause "that which you/one confess(s)"; the verb "confessi" is present-subjunctive/relative in form but functions to mean "what is confessed".
segments.purgatorio-31-g42: "non fora men nota la colpa tua" is an older word order: "non fora" is an archaic conditional/subjunctive of essere meaning "would be not" (i.e. "would not be"); "men" = "less"; "nota" = "known/noticed"; together: "your fault would not be less known" (i.e. remaining just as evident).
segments.purgatorio-31-g43: "da tal giudice sassi!" is an exclamatory formula of curse or condemnation: "da tal giudice" = "by such a judge" and "sassi" (plural of sasso, "stones") is used elliptically to wish that the person be stoned — a punchy, ironic imprecation. Word order and punctuation make it an abrupt closing threat.
segments.purgatorio-31-g44: Ma = 'but' introduces contrast. quando + present indicative scoppia ('when... bursts') gives a general temporal clause. de la propria gota literally 'from the own cheek' (propria = reflexive/possessive 'one's own'); gota here is a bodily image (cheek / tear-area). l’accusa del peccato is the subject that follows and completes the temporal clause.
segments.purgatorio-31-g45: In nostra corte is a locative phrase ('in our court'). rivolge sé is reflexive usage of rivolge ('turns/turns itself' or 'directs itself'); Dante places the verbal phrase before the grammatical subject (la rota) for poetic inversion, so la rota is the subject moved to the end. contra ’l taglio = 'against the cut' (contra + article + noun).
segments.purgatorio-31-g46: Tuttavia is an adversative adverb meaning 'nevertheless'/'still' and serves to soften or qualify what follows; it's a sentence-initial discourse marker.
segments.purgatorio-31-g47: Perché here introduces a purpose or result ('so that' / 'because'); mo = archaic 'now'. vergogna porte is an archaic verbal construction: porte is a subjunctive/optative form of portare (Dante spelling) meaning 'you may bear' or 'you should wear/bear'; grammatically, vergogna is the object borne ('shame'). del tuo errore = 'of your error'. Note: the word token 'porte' is printed here as a form that looks like the noun 'porte' but functions as a verb in context.
segments.purgatorio-31-g48: e perché altra volta links another purpose clause: perché = 'so that'/'in order that' (introducing purpose), altra volta = 'another time' or 'on another occasion'. The clause sets up a future/possible situation.
segments.purgatorio-31-g49: Udendo is a present gerund (or gerund-like participle) from udire meaning 'in hearing' or 'when hearing'. le serene is the direct object of udendo; 'le serene' is a poetic plural noun phrase (serene = 'serene/clear/calm [things or voices]') and is what, when heard, will have the stated effect.
segments.purgatorio-31-g50: sie is an archaic subjunctive/present form of essere (Dante uses sie where modern Italian has sia) meaning 'may be' or 'be'; più forte = 'stronger' or 'more forceful'. Together it expresses result/potential: 'you may be stronger.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g51: Pon giù is an imperative instructing the addressee: pon (from porre, 'put/place') + giù ('down') = 'lay down' or 'put aside'. il seme del piangere = literal 'the seed of weeping' (a figurative phrase meaning the beginning/seminar of tears/grief). e ascolta is an imperative 'and listen.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g52: sì udirai is future indicative 'thus/so you will hear.' come introduces an indirect question/comparison 'how' or 'in what way.' in contraria parte = 'on the contrary side' or 'in the opposite direction.' mover dovieti is an inverted, archaic/modal expression: mover (infinitive 'to move') + dovieti (an old-form modal meaning roughly 'you ought to / would have to'); here the ordering and archaic forms express what would have been required. mia carne sepolta = 'my buried flesh/body' (object of the implied action).
segments.purgatorio-31-g53: This is a poetic inverted negative statement: Mai non (never) + verb + indirect object t’ (to you). Appresentò is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd singular of appresentare, meaning 'presented/ offered.' Piacer (an abstract noun placed at the end) is the object 'pleasure.' The order emphasizes the denial (Mai non) and the thing withheld (piacer).
segments.purgatorio-31-g54: Quanto introduces a comparative clause 'as much as / how much.' Le belle membra is the noun phrase 'the beautiful limbs.' In ch’io rinchiusa fui the relative ch’ = 'in which,' with rinchiusa (past participle, feminine) + fui (passato remoto of essere) forming a passive perfect: 'in which I was enclosed.' The feminine participle matches the speaker (a female figure).
segments.purgatorio-31-g55: E che links back to the previous relative idea: 'and which ...' So’ is an archaic/contractional form of 'sono' (they are) used here with the past participle sparte: 'are scattered.' ’n terra = in terra ('on/in earth'). The whole clause means 'and which are scattered on the earth.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g56: E se introduces a conditional 'and if...' L' sommo piacer = 'the highest / supreme pleasure' (l' = the). Sì here is 'thus/so.' Ti is the indirect object 'to you.' Fallio is an older/past form meaning 'failed' (the pleasure failed you). Per la mia morte = 'because of my death,' giving the cause for the failure.
segments.purgatorio-31-g57: Qual cosa mortale begins a rhetorical question 'what mortal thing...' Dovea is the imperfect or conditional-like past of dovere ('was to / ought to have'), used with the infinitive trarre ('to draw/pull') to mean 'would then have drawn you.' Poi = then/afterwards. Nel suo disio = 'into its desire' (i.e. into the object of its desire).
segments.purgatorio-31-g58: Ben here is an emphatic adverb 'well/indeed' strengthening the obligation expressed by dovevi. Ti is the clitic for 'yourself,' and dovevi is the imperfect of dovere ('you ought to / you should have'), expressing what the addressee should have done.
segments.purgatorio-31-g59: Per introduces the reason 'because of / by means of' lo primo strale = 'the first shaft/arrow' (a metaphorical blow), de le cose fallaci = 'of the deceptive things.' The phrase gives the motive: 'because of the first wound/temptation of deceptive things.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g60: Levar suso is an infinitive 'to lift oneself up / to rise up' (levare + adverb sus(o) 'up'). Di retro a me = 'from behind me.' Che non era più tale is a relative clause modifying 'me' (che = 'who'), with era (imperfect 'was') and più = 'any longer,' so 'who was no longer such.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g1: A vocative address: "O tu che se’..." = "O you who are..." The form se’ is an archaic contraction of sei (you are). di là dal fiume = "on the other side of the river" (di là = 'over there / on that side', dal = da + il 'from the').
segments.purgatorio-31-g2: volgendo is a present gerund (volgere) meaning "turning/directing" and modifies the main verb ricominciò; suo parlare = "his speech" (suo = possessive); a me is the indirect object "to me"; per punta is an idiomatic prepositional phrase "with a point" (literally 'by/with point'), describing the sharpness of the address.
segments.purgatorio-31-g3: che = 'which/that' links this clause to the preceding; pur = still/even; per taglio literally 'by/through a cut' (taglio = 'cut/edge', used figuratively for tone); m’era paruto acro = an analytic past construction: m' (mi) + era (imperfect of essere) + paruto (past participle of parere) = 'had seemed to me'. Note m' is an enclitic contraction of mi (to me). Acro is an archaic adjective meaning 'harsh/sharp'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g4: ricominciò is the passato remoto / simple past third person singular of ricominciare = 'he resumed' or 'he began again'; it is the finite main verb that the preceding gerund and clauses modify.
segments.purgatorio-31-g5: seguendo is a gerund (seguire) 'following' that continues the description of how he spoke; sanza is an archaic preposition meaning 'without'; cunta (from cuntare) here means 'count' or 'reckoning' — sanza cunta = 'without counting/without keeping count' (i.e. without pausing to reckon).
segments.purgatorio-31-g6: dì is the imperative singular of dire: a direct command 'say' used here vocatively within the quotation.
segments.purgatorio-31-g7: Repeated imperative dì = 'say'; se introduces an indirect question 'whether' ("say whether…"); questo = 'this'; è is the present of essere; vero = 'true'. The whole phrase = 'say whether this is true'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g8: a tanta accusa = 'to/for so great an accusation' (a = 'to/for'); tua confession = 'your confession' (confession is a feminine noun); conviene is an impersonal/implicative present of convenire meaning 'it is fitting/necessary'; esser + congiunta is an infinitive construction with past participle used adjectivally: 'to be joined' — the participle congiunta agrees with confession (feminine).
segments.purgatorio-31-g9: Era is the imperfect of essere 'was' describing a past state; la mia virtù = 'my strength/virtue' (virtù often = 'power/strength' here); tanto confusa = 'so confused/overwhelmed'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g10: che introduces a result clause 'so that'; la voce = 'the voice'; si is the reflexive pronoun used with mosse; mosse here is a past tense of muovere (he/she/it moved/stirred) — the printed senses for mosse are not the verb senses, so the correct lexical sense is given as -1 in the senses map. Grammatically, 'la voce si mosse' = 'the voice stirred/moved'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g11: e = 'and'; pria = 'before'; si spense = reflexive/passive-looking past (from spegnere) 'it died away/was quenched' (the voice stopped) — the printed lemma/senses for spense correspond to a different verb and thus are not applicable (marked -1). che ... fosse dischiusa is a temporal clause introduced by che with the imperfect subjunctive fosse + past participle dischiusa 'that it might be opened' (subjunctive used after pria 'before'), and da li organi suoi literally marks the agent/source 'by his organs' (i.e. the vocal apparatus).
segments.purgatorio-31-g12: Poco = 'little' (here meaning 'not long' or 'not much'); sofferse is a past form meaning 'he endured/suffered/hesitated' — contextually 'he did not delay/pause long.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g13: poi = 'then/afterwards'; disse is passato remoto / preterite of dire = 'he said.' The two form a simple sequence: 'after a short pause he said'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g14: Che = 'what' here introducing a direct question; pense is an archaic/poetic second-person present form equivalent to modern pensi — 'what do you think?'. The verb is present and directed to the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-31-g15: Rispondi is the imperative singular of rispondere: 'Answer [me]'; a me is the indirect object 'to me' — a direct command to the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-31-g16: ché = 'for/because'; le memorie triste = 'the sad memories' (memorie = remembrances/reputation); in te = 'in you' (i.e. within you); non sono ancor = 'are not yet' (ancor = still/yet); da l’acqua = 'by the water' (da marks the agent/source, here the purifying water); offense is an archaic adjectival/participle use meaning 'harmed/indelibly affected' (literally 'offended/hurt by the water'). Grammatically the phrase says those sad memories in you have not yet been affected by the water.
segments.purgatorio-31-g95: 'Poi' is a simple temporal adverb meaning 'then' (marks the next moment in the narrative). It stands alone here as a connective linking this line to the preceding action.
segments.purgatorio-31-g96: This is a subordinate temporal clause introduced by 'quando' ('when'); 'il cor' is the subject ('the heart'); 'virtù di fuor' is a nominal phrase meaning 'power/strength coming from outside' (word-for-word 'virtue of out'); 'rendemmi' is an archaic/poetic past form with enclitic -mi meaning 'returned to me' or 'restored me' (so the verb governs the clause and places the action in the past).
segments.purgatorio-31-g97: 'la donna' is the direct object ('the lady'); the relative 'ch’' (= che) introduces the relative clause 'che io avea trovata sola' (literally 'whom I had found alone'); 'sopra me vidi' inverts usual subject-verb order: 'vidi' is the main verb 'I saw' and 'sopra me' ('above me') is the locative phrase that precedes it for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-31-g98: 'e' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; 'dicea' is the imperfect tense ('was saying' / 'kept saying'), which gives the reported speech a continuous or repeated sense in the past.
segments.purgatorio-31-g99: Quotation begins. 'Tiemmi' is an imperative or exhortation (poetic/dialectal form) meaning 'hold me' or 'keep me' addressed to whoever is capable of supporting her; it is direct speech, present-tense commandal in force.
segments.purgatorio-31-g100: The repetition 'tiemmi' closes the exclamation. Grammatically it is the same vocative/imperative utterance as the previous segment (poetic spelling/variation); here it functions as the echoed command.
segments.purgatorio-31-g101: Sentence-final punctuation (period) marking the end of the preceding sentence; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-31-g102: 'Tratto m’avea' is a perfect/periphrastic construction: 'tratto' (past participle 'drawn' / 'dragged') + auxiliary 'avea' ('had') with the clitic 'm’' ('me') = 'she had drawn/dragged me.' 'nel fiume infin la gola' is a prepositional phrase 'into/within the river as far as the throat' where 'infin' = 'as far as/until' and 'la gola' specifies the topographic/depth limit ('the throat' = up to my throat).
segments.purgatorio-31-g103: This coordinate clause begins with 'e' ('and'); 'tirandosi' is a gerund reflexive 'drawing/pulling herself' or 'drawing along' indicating simultaneous action; 'me dietro' = 'me behind' (i.e. 'with me trailing behind'); 'sen giva' is an idiomatic, slightly archaic motion verb (s'en + giva) meaning 'she went along' or 'she proceeded'; 'sovresso l’acqua lieve come scola' is a simile: 'over the water light as a stream' where 'sovresso' = 'over/upon', 'lieve' = 'light/gentle', 'scola' here evokes a small flowing stream (poetic usage).
segments.purgatorio-31-g104: 'Quando fui presso a la beata riva' is a temporal clause: 'Quando' ('when'); 'fui' is the perfect/simple past 'I was' or 'I came' in context meaning 'I came/was near'; 'presso a' = 'near to'; 'la beata riva' = 'the blessed shore' (the landing place).
segments.purgatorio-31-g105: The quoted Latin phrase 'Asperges me' is treated as an object he hears; 'sì dolcemente udissi' uses the imperfect 'udissi' ('I heard' / 'I seemed to hear') with 'sì dolcemente' ('so sweetly') modifying it — the whole gives the sense 'I heard "Asperges me" so sweetly.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g106: 'che' introduces a result clause 'that I do not know how to remember it' — 'nol' is the clitic object 'it' with negation ('not it'); 'so' is first person present of 'sapere' used here in the sense 'I know (how)'; 'rimembrar' is an infinitive 'to remember/recall.' Together it means 'so that I cannot recall it.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g107: 'non ch’io lo scriva' is a concessive or intensifying clause: literally 'not that I write it' meaning 'much less to say I could write it down'; 'ch’' (here a contraction of 'che'/'ché') introduces the clause, 'io' is the subject, 'lo' the object 'it', and 'scriva' is the present subjunctive used after the concessive 'non' to express inability or improbability ('I could write it').
segments.purgatorio-31-g61: Non ti dovea gravar le penne in giuso: 'dovea' is the imperfect of dovere used to express obligation/expectation ('ought to' / 'should'), followed by the infinitive 'gravar' (classic infinitive ending -ar). 'Ti' is a dative pronoun ('to you' / 'for you') showing who is affected, and 'le penne' is the direct object of 'gravar' (the thing that would weigh someone down). 'in giuso' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'downwards' placed after the object for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-31-g62: ad aspettar più colpo,: 'ad' + infinitive ('aspettar') forms a purpose/goal phrase ('to wait for'). 'più' modifies 'colpo' meaning 'another/further', and 'colpo' ('blow') is the object awaited. Word order keeps the infinitival phrase tight after 'ad'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g63: o pargoletta o altra novità con sì breve uso.: 'o ... o ...' is the simple disjunctive 'or ... or ...'. 'pargoletta' here functions as a noun meaning babyish cooing/baby-talk (an archaic/poetic word), paralleled by 'altra novità' ('some other novelty'). 'con sì breve uso' means 'with so little practice/experience'—'sì' = 'so' and 'uso' = 'use/experience'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g64: Novo augelletto due o tre aspetta;: 'Novo' ('new') modifies 'augelletto' ('little bird') and the verb 'aspetta' is present indicative 3rd singular ('waits'/'awaits'). 'due o tre' ('two or three') is a numeral expression placed between subject and verb for metrical emphasis—literally 'a new little bird waits two or three.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g65: ma dinanzi da li occhi d’i pennuti rete si spiega indarno o si saetta.: 'ma' = 'but'. 'dinanzi da li occhi d’i pennuti' is a prepositional phrase ('before/in front of the eyes of the feathered ones'), where 'da' introduces the relation to the eyes. 'rete si spiega' uses reflexive 'si' to form a passive-like construction ('a net is unfolded/spread'); 'spiega' here is 'unfolds/spreads out.' 'indarno' means 'in vain.' The alternative 'o si saetta' uses the same reflexive pattern with 'saetta' (verb) meaning 'an arrow is shot/darts forth.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g66: Quali fanciulli,: 'Quali' here functions as a comparative/simile ('like' or 'such as') and introduces the simile 'like children.' 'fanciulli' is the plural noun 'children.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g67: vergognando,: present participle/gerund 'vergognando' ('being ashamed'), descriptive and modifying the children in the simile; placed after the noun phrase to add a circumstantial detail.
segments.purgatorio-31-g68: muti con li occhi a terra stannosi,: 'muti' ('silent') describes the children; the phrase 'con li occhi a terra' means 'with their eyes to the ground.' 'stannosi' is an archaic contracted form combining 'stanno' (they stand) + 'si' (reflexive/enclitic), so syntactically 'they stand (themselves) — i.e., they stand there.' Word order places the adjectives and adverbial phrases around the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.purgatorio-31-g69: ascoltando e sé riconoscendo e ripentuti,: a string of participles/past participle forms describing simultaneous conditions: 'ascoltando' (listening), 'sé riconoscendo' ('recognizing themselves' — 'sé' reflexive), and 'ripentuti' (past participle 'repentant'/'having repented'). The coordination links these internal reactions together.
segments.purgatorio-31-g70: tal mi stav’ io;: 'tal' = 'such'/'thus', 'mi' is the indirect object ('to me' or reflexive sense as experiencer), and 'stav’' is the contracted archaic form of 'stavo'/'stava' (imperfect of stare) meaning 'I was (thus)'; the enclitic apostrophe marks the dropped vowel. Overall: 'such was I' or 'thus I stood.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g71: ed ella disse:: simple narrative clause — 'ed' = 'and', 'ella' = 'she', 'disse' = preterite 'said' (3rd singular). It introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-31-g72: «Quando per udir se’ dolente,: direct speech begins. 'Quando' = 'when/whenever'; 'per udir' is purpose/occasion 'in order to hear / when hearing'; 'se’' is the contraction of 'sei' (2nd singular present of essere) used poetically — here 'you are' — and 'dolente' is a predicative adjective 'sorrowful.' So: 'When you are sorrowful to hear' (i.e., when you are pained at hearing something).
segments.purgatorio-31-g73: alza la barba,: an imperative addressed to 'you' — 'alza' is 2nd singular imperative (or present indicative used as hortative) of 'alzare' meaning 'raise/lift'; 'la barba' ('the beard') is the direct object — idiomatically 'lift your chin/beard' meaning 'look up' or 'raise your face.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g74: e prenderai più doglia riguardando».: 'e' = 'and'; 'prenderai' is future 2nd singular of 'prendere' ('you will take' / 'you will feel'), so prophetic/advisory 'you will feel more pain'; 'più doglia' = 'more sorrow/pain'; 'riguardando' is a gerund/participial form from 'riguardare' used as 'in looking / when you look back'—here causal/temporal, 'by looking' or 'when you look.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g75: Con men di resistenza = 'with less resistance.' Si dibarba is a reflexive/passive construction meaning 'is (or is made) to be shorn / deprived of its beard' (dibarbare = to shave off). Robust o cerro = 'a robust oak' (cerro = a kind of oak) is the subject being acted on; Italian permits this impersonal/passive phrasing.
segments.purgatorio-31-g76: O vero (or indeed) introduces alternatives: 'either to our wind or to that of the land of grass.' Al nostral vento = 'to our wind'; a quel de la terra di Iarba = 'to that of the grassy earth' (Iarba = grass/herbage). The repetition o vero gives a rhetorical cadence.
segments.purgatorio-31-g77: Ch’io non levai al suo comando il mento is a subordinate clause 'that I did not raise my chin at his command.' Ch’ = 'that/which,' io = 'I,' non + levai (passato remoto 'I raised' negated) = 'I did not raise,' al suo comando = 'at his command,' il mento = 'the chin.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g78: E quando introduces a temporal clause 'and when...' Per la barba = 'by/through the beard' (the beard being the means), il viso chiese = 'the face sought/asked' (chiese = passato remoto 'asked/required'). Grammatically this is figurative: the face 'asked' or 'sought' via the beard.
segments.purgatorio-31-g79: Ben conobbi = 'I well/perceptively recognized' (conobbi = passato remoto 'I knew/recognized'). Il velen de l’argomento = 'the poison/venom of the argument' i.e. the harmful quality of that reasoning; argomento here means 'argument/reasoning.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g151: This is a vocative address: O + noun phrase. Isplendor (archaic spelling of 'splendore') is the head noun; the prepositional phrase di viva luce etterna modifies it, with viva and etterna as adjectives that follow the noun they describe in this poetic order (viva modifies luce; etterna qualifies the whole phrase). The whole phrase names a divine, living light and is set off as the addressee of the question that follows.
segments.purgatorio-31-g152: This clause begins a relative/exclamatory question introduced by chi ('who'). Palido is an adjective; si fece uses the reflexive si with passato remoto fece of fare used here to mean 'became' ('made himself pale'). Sotto l'ombra di Parnaso is a prepositional phrase 'under the shade of Parnassus'; sì before di Parnaso functions as an intensifier ('so/thus') in the poetic syntax.
segments.purgatorio-31-g153: The o at the start is the disjunctive 'or', offering an alternative action. Bevve is passato remoto of bevere ('to drink'), with an implied subject; in sua cisterna ('in/at its cistern') is a locative phrase indicating where the drinking took place. The short clause contrasts two possible explanations of the paleness.
segments.purgatorio-31-g154: Che introduces a subordinate clause describing purpose or result: 'so that...'. Paresse is the imperfect subjunctive of parere ('to seem') used here in a counterfactual/result sense ('so that it would not seem'). Aver is the infinitive 'to have' governing la mente ingombra ('the mind encumbered'), where ingombra functions as an adjective meaning 'cluttered/obstructed'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g155: Tentando is a gerund 'attempting'; it is followed by the preposition a plus the infinitive render (Dante's form of 'rendere') meaning 'to make/depict/represent' and its object te ('you'). Qual tu paresti is a comparative/quotative phrase 'as you appeared' (paresti is a past-form meaning 'appeared/resembled' in the narrative context). Là dove introduces a locative relative 'there where'; armonizzando is a gerund ('harmonizing') that parentheses the verb phrase, and t’adombra is adombra (present tense 'shades/overshadows') with the clitic pronoun t' attached, meaning 'it overshadows you.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g156: Quando 'when' introduces the temporal question. Ne l'aere aperto is a frozen locative phrase 'in the open air' (ne + l' + aere aperto). Ti is the clitic reflexive/object pronoun 'you', placed before the verb; solvesti is the passato remoto (2nd person singular) of solvere used reflexively here to mean 'you loosed/released/were unbound' (i.e. were revealed or freed) in that open-air moment.
segments.purgatorio-31-g108: Subject + phrase: 'La bella donna' is the noun phrase subject ('the beautiful lady'). 'ne le braccia' means 'into/among her arms' (ne = 'into them' / 'in them'); 'aprissi' is a poetic/archaic past form meaning 'opened' (third-person past). The clause thus says she opened her arms toward/for him.
segments.purgatorio-31-g109: 'abbracciommi' is the past historic third-person form with the enclitic 'mi' attached (she embraced me). 'la testa' is the direct object ('the head'). 'e mi sommerse' continues with another passato remoto verb 'sommerse' (she submerged/overwhelmed me). 'ove convenne ch’io l’acqua inghiottissi' is an adverbial relative/temporal clause: 'ove' = 'where', 'convenne' (impersonal/past) = 'it was necessary/it befell', followed by 'ch’io ... inghiottissi' (imperfect subjunctive) = 'that I should swallow' — the subjunctive marks necessity/obligation in subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-31-g110: 'Indi' is an adverb 'then/thence'. 'mi tolse' is passato remoto (third-person) of 'togliere' with clitic 'mi' = 'she took me away' or 'she took me'. The clause is a simple past action following the previous scene.
segments.purgatorio-31-g111: Compound clause: 'e' = 'and'. 'bagnato' is a past participle ('having bathed / wet'), here used with an implied subject (she) to mean 'having bathed me'. 'm’offerse' = 'offrì mi' (passato remoto of 'offrire' with enclitic 'mi'): 'she presented/offered me'. 'dentro a la danza de le quattro belle' = 'into the dance of the four beautiful ones' (dentro a = 'into/within').
segments.purgatorio-31-g112: 'e ciascuna' = 'and each one'. 'del braccio' = 'of the arm' (i.e. by her arm). 'mi coperse' is passato remoto of 'coprire' with clitic 'mi' = 'she covered me' (each of them covered me with her arm). Word order marks the agent-of-action as each individual of the four.
segments.purgatorio-31-g113: 'Noi siam' uses the contracted present indicative 'siam' (poetic form of 'siamo') — 'we are'. 'qui ninfe' = 'here nymphs'. 'e nel ciel siamo stelle' links by 'and' and repeats the verb 'siamo' for emphasis: 'and in heaven we are stars.' The clause contrasts earthly/mythic role and celestial role.
segments.purgatorio-31-g114: 'pria che' is a temporal conjunction meaning 'before'. It takes the imperfect subjunctive 'discendesse' (discendere) after it: 'before Beatrice descended to the world' — the subjunctive marks the subordinate temporal clause. 'al mondo' = 'to the world' (i.e. to earthly life).
segments.purgatorio-31-g115: 'fummo' is passato remoto (first-person plural) of 'essere' used here as 'we were'. 'ordinate' is the past participle (feminine plural) meaning 'ordained/assigned/appointed'. 'a lei' = 'to her' and 'per sue ancelle' = 'as her handmaids' (literally 'for her handmaids'). The sequence expresses a past appointment to service.
segments.purgatorio-31-g116: 'Merrenti' is a present participle agreeing with the speaker-group ('we/they pleasing') and here carries the sense 'pleasing/deserving' to her eyes; 'a li occhi suoi' = 'to her eyes'. The construction is tersely poetic: 'Pleasing to her eyes.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g117: Contrast introduced by 'ma' ('but'). 'nel giocondo lume' = 'in the joyful/pleasant light'; 'ch’è dentro' is a relative clause 'that is within/inside'. 'aguzzeranno' is future indicative (third-person plural) of 'aguzzare' = 'they will sharpen'. Word order places the object 'i tuoi' ('your [eyes]') before the subject phrase 'le tre di là' for poetic emphasis: the three over there will sharpen your (eyes).
segments.purgatorio-31-g118: 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'le tre di là'. 'miran' is a poetic present tense form (third-person plural) = 'they look' (modern Italian 'mirano'). 'più profondo' = 'more deeply/profoundly' — the clause as a whole: 'which look more deeply.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g17: This is a noun phrase (Confusione e paura) joined by e and qualified by the participial adjective miste (agreeing in number/gender with the compound subject); it is the subject of the verb pinsero. Pinsero is passato remoto, 3rd person plural, meaning “they pressed/forced,” with the indirect object mi (“me”). Un tal “sì” is the direct object (‘such a “yes”’) and fuor de la bocca uses the archaic prepositional phrase fuor de (= fuori da) to mean “out of the mouth.”
segments.purgatorio-31-g18: Al quale is a relative phrase referring back to that “sì” (literally ‘to which’). Intender is an infinitive ‘to understand/interpret’; it appears in an impersonal/necessitative construction with fuor mestier (= ‘it was necessary’ — an archaic phrasing). The logical subject of intender is le viste (‘the sights’ / ‘the eyes’), placed after the verb-phrase, so the sense is ‘that had to be understood by the eyes.’
segments.purgatorio-31-g19: Come introduces a simile (‘as’). Balestro is the subject (‘a crossbow’). Frange is 3rd person singular present indicative of frangere (Dante uses the present for a general fact): ‘breaks.’ The whole is the opening of the simile: ‘As a crossbow breaks…’
segments.purgatorio-31-g20: Quando scocca is a temporal clause ‘when it is released/shot’ (scocca = scoccare, ‘to be shot/released’). Da troppa tesa uses da + noun to indicate cause/origin: ‘from/through too great tension’ (tesa = ‘taut/tension’). The clause explains the condition under which the crossbow breaks.
segments.purgatorio-31-g21: A simple coordinated noun phrase: la sua corda e l’arco = ‘its string and the bow.’ Sua is the possessive agreeing with corda; l’ is the elided article before arco.
segments.purgatorio-31-g22: E con men foga is an ablative/means phrase ‘and with less force’ (men = archaic meno). L’asta is the subject (‘the shaft’), il segno the object (‘the mark/target’), and tocca is 3rd person singular present ‘touches/reaches.’ Word order places the instrument phrase first for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-31-g23: Sì links the simile to the speaker (‘so, thus’). Scoppia’ io is an archaic/past form (scoppiai) meaning ‘I burst’ or ‘I broke out’ — first person, past. Sottesso is a fused form meaning ‘under that’ (sotto + esso); grave carco is an adjectival phrase ‘heavy-laden’ describing the load under which he burst.
segments.purgatorio-31-g24: Fuori + gerundio (sgorgando) = ‘out, gushing forth’; lagrime e sospiri are the things gushing out (‘tears and sighs’). The gerund expresses simultaneous action accompanying 'I burst.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g25: E la voce allentò: allentò is passato remoto, 3rd singular of allentare ‘loosened’ or ‘eased’; la voce is the subject (‘my voice’/‘the voice’). Per lo suo varco = ‘through its passage/opening’ (varco = opening/passage, here the mouth), indicating the channel through which the voice came.
segments.purgatorio-31-g26: "Ond’" is a contracted, poetic form of onde/ond(e) meaning "thereupon" or "then"; "ella a me" is simple subject + indirect object: "she to me" (i.e. "she said to me"), with the verb implied by the colon and the following speech.
segments.purgatorio-31-g27: "Per entro" is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase meaning "through/within"; "i mie' disiri" is "my desires" with the apostrophe marking elision (mie') for the hexameter. The whole phrase functions adverbially to locate the subsequent clause inside those desires.
segments.purgatorio-31-g28: This is a relative clause modifying "i mie' disiri": "che ti menavano" uses the imperfect "menavano" (they were leading/used to lead) describing an ongoing past action; "ad amar" is the infinitive of purpose "to love"; "lo bene" = "the Good" (abstract noun). "di là dal qual" = "beyond which" and "non è a che s'aspiri" is an impersonal construction with subjunctive "s'aspiri" meaning "there is nothing to be aspired to" (literally: "there is nothing to which one aspires").
segments.purgatorio-31-g29: "quai fossi attraversati" and "quai catene trovasti" are two parallel interrogative phrases: "quai" (which/what) introduces the question; "fossi attraversati" is an archaic/periphrastic form implying things that had to be crossed (passive sense); "attraversati" is a past participle meaning "crossed"; "quai catene trovasti" = "what chains did you find" ("trovasti" = archaic past tense "you found"). The two items name obstacles encountered.
segments.purgatorio-31-g30: "per che" here introduces the consequence or cause-question ("wherefore/why"); "del passare innanzi" is a noun-phrase "of the passing ahead" (del = di + il); "dovessiti" is an archaic second-person form of the conditional/necessitative auxiliary ("you had to" / "you ought to have"); "così spogliar la spene" uses the bare infinitive "spogliar" (to strip away) and the abstract noun "la spene" = "the hope" (archaic spelling), so the clause asks why you were obliged to strip away your hope in order to pass ahead.
segments.purgatorio-31-g31: "E" = "and"; "quali agevolezze o quali avanzi" = "what easements (advantages) or what remnants/advantages" — two parallel interrogative nouns. "ne la fronte de li altri" literally "in/on the front of the others" idiomatically meaning their presence/face or the precedence shown by others. "si mostraro" is a poetic past tense (3rd pl.) with the reflexive/pronominal "si", meaning "appeared/showed themselves".
segments.purgatorio-31-g32: The clause "per che dovessi lor passeggiare anzi?" asks for the reason: "per che" = "wherefore/why"; "dovessi" is the imperfect subjunctive or conditional-like form meaning "should you have to" / "would you have to"; "lor" = "them/to them"; "passeggiare" literally "to walk/strut" but here idiomatically means "to pass by/precede"; "anzi" here functions "before/first". The whole asks why you had to go ahead of them.
segments.purgatorio-31-g119: Così (adverb) = 'thus/so' and introduces manner. cantando is a present gerund of cantare ('singing') describing how they began. cominciaro is an archaic past-tense form (passato remoto) meaning 'they began' — Dante's third-person plural ending -aro instead of modern -arono.
segments.purgatorio-31-g120: e poi = 'and then/afterwards' (e = and; poi = then). al petto del grifon = 'to the breast/chest of the griffin' (al = a + il; petto = chest; del = di + il). seco = 'with him' (old enclitic adverb). menarmi is the infinitive menare + enclitic -mi ('to lead/take me') and functions as the complement of cominciaro ('they began to lead me').
segments.purgatorio-31-g121: ove = 'where'. Beatrice is the proper name. stava is the imperfect of stare ('was, stood, was situated'), marking a continuing state. volta (literally 'turned/turning') functions as an adjective/participle: 'stava volta' = 'was turned'. a noi = 'toward us/ facing us'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g122: Disser is the archaic passato remoto third-person plural of dire ('they said'); the colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-31-g123: Fa is the imperative of fare ('do, make') used to give the order. che introduces a subordinate clause with the present subjunctive. le viste = 'the sights/the gaze' (subject of the subordinate clause). non risparmi is subjunctive (non + risparmi) here meaning 'do not spare'/'do not hold back' — risparmi is used in the subjunctive/imperative sense 'spare' (Dante's morphology may differ from modern forms).
segments.purgatorio-31-g124: posto here functions as 'since/considering that' (literally 'placed/given' but used conjunctively). t' + avem = t'avem = 'we have you/we have placed you' (avem = abbiamo/we have). dinanzi = 'in front of, before'. a li smeraldi = 'before the emeralds' (a + il). ond' (ond') = 'whence/from which'. Amor is the personified Love. già = 'already'. ti = 'you' (object). trasse is passato remoto of trarre ('he/she/it drew'); le sue armi = 'his/its arms/weapons.' Together: 'since we have placed you before the emeralds from which Love already drew his arms.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g125: Mille disiri = 'a thousand desires/longings'. più che fiamma caldi = comparative phrase 'hotter than flame' modifying the desires. strinsermi is an archaic enclitic past form: strinsero (they pressed) + -mi ('me') combined to give 'they pressed me' (i.e. pressed my eyes against). li occhi a li occhi rilucenti = 'my eyes to the shining eyes' (li = the; occhi = eyes; a = to; rilucenti = shining).
segments.purgatorio-31-g126: che = 'which/that' introducing a relative clause referring back to the shining eyes. pur = 'still/nevertheless'. sopra ’l grifone = 'upon the griffin' (sopra + l = sopra il). stavan is the imperfect ('they were standing/they remained') used here to describe a continuing state. saldi = 'firm/steady.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g127: Come = 'as/like'. in lo specchio = 'in the mirror' (in + lo + specchio). il sol here is the poetic expression 'the sun' (il sol) — note: the provided sense list did not include 'sun', so that specific lexical sense is not represented in the supplied options.
segments.purgatorio-31-g128: non altrimenti = 'not otherwise/ no differently'. la doppia fiera = 'the double/double-faced beast' (the twofold creature, i.e. the griffin composed of two natures). dentro = 'inside/within'. vi = 'there/in it' (adverbial). raggiava is the imperfect of raggiare ('it was radiating/it shone forth'), describing continuous shining.
segments.purgatorio-31-g129: or = 'now/then' (a temporal particle indicating alternation). con altri = 'with others' (con = with; altri = others), introducing the first of two alternating manners.
segments.purgatorio-31-g130: or (again) = 'now' marking the alternating counterpart. con altri reggimenti = 'with other regimens/directions/arrangements' — reggimenti here means kinds of arrangement, movement, or modes of display (not military regiments), so the phrase means 'now by one mode, now by another.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g131: Imperative second-person singular of 'pensare' addressed to the reader; standalone as a vocative command introducing what the reader should consider.
segments.purgatorio-31-g132: Vocative noun 'lettor' (reader) in familiar/formal address; archaic spelling without final 'e' but functioning as direct addressee.
segments.purgatorio-31-g133: Contains a contraction 's’' (here standing for 'se', i.e. 'if' or 'whether') followed by 'io' (subject) and the reflexive object pronoun 'mi'; 'maravigliava' is imperfect indicative of the reflexive verb 'maravigliarsi', so literally 'I was marvelling' / 'I was astonished'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g134: 'quando' introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'); 'vedea' is an imperfect (1st person) form of 'vedere' used here as 'I saw' or 'I perceived' in narrative; 'la cosa' is the direct object; 'in sé' is a reflexive/emphatic locution meaning 'in itself'; 'star' is the infinitive 'stare' used after perception to mean 'to be' or 'to remain'; 'queta' is an adjective (feminine) meaning 'quiet/calm' modifying 'cosa'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g135: 'e' = 'and' linking clauses; 'ne' here means 'in' or 'within' (indicating location/containment); 'l’ idolo suo' = 'its/its own idol' (article + noun + possessive), with poetic order; 'si' is reflexive marking that the subject (the idol) is affected by the verb; 'trasmutava' is the imperfect of 'trasmutare' meaning 'was transformed' or 'was changing', so the idol itself was undergoing transformation.
segments.purgatorio-31-g136: 'Mentre che' = 'while' (temporal subordinating phrase); 'piena di stupore e lieta' is a participial/adjectival phrase modifying 'l’anima mia' (my soul) — 'full of wonder and joyful'; 'l’anima mia' is the subject; 'gustava' is the imperfect of 'gustare' meaning 'was tasting' or 'was enjoying'; 'di quel cibo' is the object ('of that food'); the final 'che' begins a relative clause modifying 'cibo'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g137: 'saziando' is a present gerund of 'saziare' meaning 'satiating' or 'satisfying'; 'di sé' ('of itself') indicates the thing's own self as the object of the satiation — a reflexive/genitive sense: 'satisfying itself'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g138: Repeats the reflexive 'di sé' ('of itself'); 'asseta' is an archaic or dialectal form here whose intended meaning in context is 'is made thirsty' or 'is still thirsty' (paradoxical: satisfying itself yet made thirsty), though the supplied sense list does not show that sense, so the form is irregular/poetic.
segments.purgatorio-31-g139: 'sé dimostrando' = 'showing itself' (sé reflexive; 'dimostrando' gerund/participle of 'dimostrare' = 'show'); 'di più alto tribo' is a prepositional phrase: 'di' + comparative 'più' + adjective 'alto' modifying 'tribo' (a rare/poetic noun here); 'ne li atti' = 'in the acts' (ne = in, li = the, atti = acts), so the clause says the thing showed itself of a higher order in its actions.
segments.purgatorio-31-g140: 'l’altre tre' = 'the other three' (article + adjective + numeral used substantively); 'si fero avanti' is an archaic/poetic past expression meaning 'made themselves advance' or 'stepped forward' (the form 'fero' here is irregular or dialectal for 'fecero'/'feceron'); reflexive 'si' marks that they performed the action upon themselves/moved themselves forward; 'avanti' = 'forward'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g141: 'danzando' is a present participle/gerund 'dancing'; 'al loro' = 'to their' (a + il omitted because 'al' used before 'loro' in older usage here means 'to the [something] of theirs'); 'angelico' is an adjective 'angelic'; 'caribo' is a poetic noun (likely 'caro'/'carol' or 'caribo' = 'dear/cherished song') functioning as the object of dancing 'to their angelic carol'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g142: Imperative form 'Volgi' (second-person singular) of 'volgere' meaning 'turn' or 'direct' addressed to Beatrice; used as the verb of a vocative invocation.
segments.purgatorio-31-g143: Vocative proper name 'Beatrice' directly addressed; standard modern spelling would be capitalized as here.
segments.purgatorio-31-g144: Again 'volgi' (imperative) followed by the direct object 'li occhi' ('the eyes' with archaic plural article 'li'); 'santi' is an adjective 'holy/saintly' modifying 'occhi'. Note: the lemma for 'volgi' supplied in the data is inconsistent, but syntactically this is the imperative 'turn'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g145: 'era' is the imperfect of 'essere' ('was') and here serves as the copula introducing an indirect statement; 'la sua canzone' = 'their song' or 'its song' (article + possessive + noun) — 'era la sua canzone' = 'that was their/its song' (i.e., that was what they sang).
segments.purgatorio-31-g146: 'al tuo fedele' = 'to your faithful one' (a + il = al; 'tuo' possessive; 'fedele' as substantive meaning 'the faithful one' or 'devoted follower'); the following 'che' begins a relative clause ('who...').
segments.purgatorio-31-g147: 'per' here expresses purpose ('in order to' / 'to'); 'vederti' is the clitic infinitive 'vedere' + enclitic object 'ti' = 'to see you' (infinitive of purpose: 'who, to see you...').
segments.purgatorio-31-g148: 'ha' is the present indicative of 'avere' used as an auxiliary in a compound construction; 'mossi' is the past participle of 'muovere' (here used transitively: 'has moved/has taken'); 'passi' is plural noun 'steps'; 'tanti' is an adjective meaning 'so many' — together 'has taken so many steps' (i.e. 'has traveled so far').
segments.purgatorio-31-g149: 'Per grazia' = 'For/by grace' (prepositional phrase giving motive); 'fa' is third-person singular of 'fare' meaning 'do/make' here used impersonally 'grant' or 'bestow'; 'noi' is the indirect object 'us'; the following 'grazia' (in the supplied senses is treated as the verb sense of 'graziare') expresses the favor to be given; 'che disvele a lui la bocca tua' is a subordinate clause: 'that unveils to him your mouth' — 'disvele' present indicative of 'disvelare' = 'reveal/unveil', 'a lui' ('to him'), 'la bocca tua' = 'your mouth' (i.e. your speech or the openness of your mouth).
segments.purgatorio-31-g150: 'sì che' functions as a result clause meaning 'so that'; 'discerna' is present subjunctive/indicative of 'discernere' ('may/must discern' or simply 'discerns' depending on reading) governing 'la seconda bellezza' = 'the second beauty'; the final 'che tu cele' is a relative clause: 'that you cele' — the verb form 'cele' (from an archaic/poetic verb) is irregular in the supplied senses (likely 'celare' = 'to hide'), so syntactically it is 'that you [do X to]' and here contextually 'that you hide'.
segments.purgatorio-31-g80: E = conjunction 'and'. Come here introduces a temporal clause, meaning 'when/as'. La mia faccia si distese: reflexive verb construction (si + distese) in Dante’s past tense meaning 'my face relaxed/grew smooth' or 'fell back'; distese is a past (poetic/archaic) form of distendere. Word order is subject (la mia faccia) then reflexive verb (si distese).
segments.purgatorio-31-g81: Posarsi quelle prime creature: posarsi is reflexive 'to settle/sit themselves' with subject quelle prime creature placed after the verb for poetic inversion; read as a past action 'settled themselves'. Da loro aspersïon l’occhio comprese: 'da' = 'from/by', 'loro aspersion' = 'their sprinkling (aspersion)', and comprese is past tense (passato remoto) of comprendere used here in the sense 'covered/embraced' the eye (l’occhio). The two clauses are coordinated in this single line.
segments.purgatorio-31-g82: E = 'and' linking the previous clause. Le mie luci = 'my lights' (a common poetic/metrical expression for 'my eyes' or 'my sight'): definite article + possessive + plural noun agreeing (le mie luci).
segments.purgatorio-31-g83: Ancor = archaic form of ancora, 'still' or 'yet'. Poco = 'a little'. Sicure (feminine plural to agree with luci) = 'certain/steady' but here, with poco/ancor, it means 'still a little unsure/unstable'. The phrase modifies le mie luci.
segments.purgatorio-31-g84: Vider is Dante's (poetic) past form of vedere used here as a past tense verb 'saw' whose subject is the preceding le mie luci (pl.). Beatrice is the direct object. Volta in su = 'turned upward/toward' (volta is a participial/nominal form meaning 'turned' or 'turned face'); in + su = 'up/toward upwards'. La fiera = 'the beast' (feminine). Ch’ è sola una persona in due nature is a relative clause: ch’ = che 'which/that', è = 'is', sola = 'only/alone', una persona = 'one person' and in due nature = 'in two natures' (theological phrase).
segments.purgatorio-31-g85: Sotto 'l suo velo e oltre la rivera = two prepositional phrases 'under her veil and beyond the riverbank/shore' describing where/under what impression. Vincer pariemi più sé stessa antica: vincer (infinitive) 'to prevail/overcome/surpass' here expresses the notion of prevailing; pariemi functions like an impersonal/archaic 'parve a me' (it seemed to me) — the whole reads 'it seemed to me she prevailed more as her ancient self' with sé stessa antica = 'her older/ancient self'. (Note: pariemi is an archaic/poetic form; treat it as 'seemed to me' in sense.)
segments.purgatorio-31-g86: Vincer = 'to prevail' again; che here is being used in the comparative sense 'than'. L’altre qui = 'the others here' (other persons present in this scene). The phrase expresses comparison: 'she prevailed more than the others here.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g87: Quand' = quando 'when' (elided). Ella = 'she'. C’era is ci + era = 'was there' (imperfect era = 'was'); together 'when she was there/present.' The clitic ci indicates location ('there').
segments.purgatorio-31-g88: Di penter = 'from/of repentance' (di + infinitive/verb-noun pentere = repentance), i.e. indicating cause or reference to repentance. Sì = 'so' (intensifier). Mi = indirect object 'me'. Punse = passato remoto of pungere 'stung/pricked'. Ivi = 'there'. L’ortica = 'the nettle' (subject, feminine). The verb is placed before the subject for emphasis (punse ivi l'ortica = the nettle stung me there).
segments.purgatorio-31-g89: Che = 'so/because/that' introducing the result/explanation clause. Di tutte altre cose = 'of all other things'. Qual = 'which/that (one)' referring to whichever thing most affected him. Mi = 'to me'. Torse is contextually a past form meaning 'twisted' or 'turned' (here 'turned/tore me' emotionally) — an archaic/poetic past; it functions as a past verb meaning something like 'twisted me' or 'caused me pain/turning'. Più nel suo amor = 'more in her love' i.e. 'in regard to his/her love' (comparing degree).
segments.purgatorio-31-g90: Più = 'more'. Mi = 'to me'. Si fé = si + fé: reflexive construction of fare in the past (fe = fe' / fece variant), 'made itself/turned itself' or 'became (for me)'. Nemica = 'hostile/enemy' (feminine to agree with l'ortica or the thing that became hostile). So 'it became more of an enemy to me.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g91: Tanta = 'so much'. Riconoscenza = 'gratitude/recognition' (object of tanta). Il cor = 'the heart' (subject). Mi = 'at me/to me' (indirect object). Morse = passato remoto of mordere 'bit/gnawed/ate at' — 'my heart gnawed me' i.e. 'my heart pricked/tormented me.' Word order is emphatic: measure + noun + subject + object + verb.
segments.purgatorio-31-g92: Ch’ = che 'so that/that'. Io = 'I'. Caddi = passato remoto of cadere 'I fell'. Vinto = past participle of vincere used adjectivally 'defeated/overcome' — together 'I fell, overcome.'
segments.purgatorio-31-g93: E = 'and'. Quale = exclamatory/indefinite 'what a/which kind of'. Allora = 'then'. Femmi is an archaic form of femmina/femma 'woman' used predicatively — here 'what a woman I was then' or 'in what a womanly state I was then' (an exclamatory description of his condition).
segments.purgatorio-31-g94: Salsi is an archaic/poetic past-form here meaning 'rose/rose up' or 'stood up' (the text has an uncommon form; functionally it is a past tense verb indicating motion). Colei = 'she who' / 'the one (woman)'. Che = relative 'who/that'. La cagion mi porse: la cagion = 'the cause/reason'; mi = 'to me'; porse = passato remoto of porgere 'held out, offered' — 'she who offered me the cause.' Grammatically, colei is the subject of porse; salsi agrees with colei as the preceding action ('she rose/stepped forward').

sentences.purgatorio-31-s10: After the drawing-out of a bitter sigh, I could scarcely find the voice that answered, and my lips with difficulty formed it.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s11: Weeping I said: 'These present things, by their false pleasure, turned my steps as soon as your face was hidden.'
sentences.purgatorio-31-s12: And she: "If I were to be silent, or if I were to deny what I confess, your guilt would not be less known; may you be stoned by such a judge!"
sentences.purgatorio-31-s13: But when, from its own cheek, the accusation of sin bursts forth, in our court the wheel turns itself against the cut.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s14: Nevertheless, so that you may now wear the shame of your error, and so that another time, hearing the serene things, you may be the stronger, put down the seed of weeping and listen: thus you will hear how, on the contrary side, my buried body would have to move.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s15: Never did nature or art present you with pleasure as much as the beautiful limbs in which I was enclosed, and which are now scattered on earth; and if the supreme pleasure thus failed you because of my death, what mortal thing then could have drawn you into its desire?
sentences.purgatorio-31-s16: You should indeed have raised yourself up, because of the first shaft of deceptive things, from behind me, who was no longer such.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s1: "O you who are on the other side of the sacred river," turning his speech to me with a point, which even by its cutting had seemed sharp to me, he resumed, following without counting, "say, say whether this is true: your confession must be joined to so great an accusation."
sentences.purgatorio-31-s2: My strength was so confused that the voice moved, and it died away before it could be opened from his organs.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s3: He did not pause long; then he said: "What do you think?"
sentences.purgatorio-31-s4: Answer me; for the sad memories in you have not yet been harmed by the water."
sentences.purgatorio-31-s25: Then, when the power of my heart came back to me from without, I saw the lady whom I had found alone above me, and she kept saying, "Hold me, hold me!"
sentences.purgatorio-31-s27: She had dragged me in the river up as far as my throat, and, drawing me along behind her, she went with me over the water as lightly as a stream.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s28: When I came near the blessed shore, I heard 'Asperges me' so sweetly that I cannot recall it, much less write it down.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s17: Your feathers should not have been weighed down, to wait for another blow, or for babyish cooing or some other novelty with so little practice.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s18: A new little bird waits two or three; but before the eyes of the feathered ones a net is spread in vain, or an arrow is shot.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s19: Like children, ashamed, they stand silent with their eyes to the ground, listening and recognizing themselves and repentant; such was I; and she said: 'When you are sorrowful to hear, raise your beard, and you will take more pain in looking.'
sentences.purgatorio-31-s20: A sturdy oak is more easily stripped of its beard, whether by our wind or by that of the grassy earth; I did not raise my chin at his command; and when the face sought (something) by the beard, I well perceived the poison of the argument.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s41: O splendour of living, eternal light, who grew pale beneath the shade of Parnassus, or drank from its cistern, so that it would not seem to have its mind encumbered, trying to render you as you appeared there where, harmonizing, heaven overshadows you, when in the open air you unloosed yourself?
sentences.purgatorio-31-s29: The beautiful lady opened her arms to me; she embraced my head and submerged me where it was fitting that I should swallow the water.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s30: Then she took me; and, having bathed me, she offered me into the dance of the four fair ones; and each one covered me with her arm.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s31: “We are here nymphs, and in heaven we are stars; before Beatrice descended to the world, we were ordained to her as her handmaids.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s32: They please her eyes; but in the joyous light that is within, the three over there, which look more deeply, will sharpen yours.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s5: Confusion and fear, mixed together, forced such a “yes” out of my mouth, which the eyes had to interpret.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s6: As a crossbow breaks its string and bow when it is released from excessive tension, and the shaft reaches the mark with less force, so I burst—under that heavy load—pouring forth tears and sighs, and my voice loosened through its opening.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s7: Thereupon she said to me: "Through my desires that were leading you to love the Good — the Good beyond which there is nothing to aspire to — what ditches did you have to cross or what chains did you find, that for passing ahead you had to strip away your hope like this?"
sentences.purgatorio-31-s8: And what easements or what advantages showed themselves on the foreheads of the others, so that you should pass them by first?
sentences.purgatorio-31-s33: Thus, singing, they began; and then (to) lead me with him to the griffin's breast, where Beatrice was turned toward us.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s34: They said: "Do that the gaze spare nothing; since we have placed you before the emeralds from which Love already drew his arms."
sentences.purgatorio-31-s35: A thousand longings, hotter than flame, pressed my eyes to those shining eyes, which nonetheless stood firm upon the griffin.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s36: As the sun in a mirror, no differently did the twofold beast shine within it, now with one set of displays, now with another.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s37: Think, reader, whether I was astonished, when I saw the thing remain quiet in itself, and in its own idol it was transformed.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s38: While my soul, full of wonder and joyful, was tasting that nourishment which, by satisfying itself, made itself thirsty; showing itself of a higher order in its acts, the other three made themselves forward, dancing to their angelic carol.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s39: "Turn, Beatrice, turn your holy eyes," was their song, "to your faithful one who, to see you, has set out so many steps!"
sentences.purgatorio-31-s40: By grace, grant us grace that unveils to him your mouth, so that he may discern the second beauty that you hide.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s21: And when my face relaxed, those first creatures settled themselves; the sprinkling from them covered my eye; and my eyes, still a little uncertain, saw Beatrice turned up toward the beast which is only one person in two natures.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s22: Beneath her veil and beyond the bank it seemed to me she prevailed more as her ancient self, more than the others here when she was present.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s23: The nettle there pricked me so with repentance that, of all other things that had twisted me most in her love, it became more hostile to me.
sentences.purgatorio-31-s24: So much gratitude gnawed at my heart that I fell overcome; and what a woman I was then — she rose who had offered me the cause.
